design_philosophy.core_loop: Drive armed jeep through enemy territory, rescue POWs from camps, extract them via helicopter, upgrade weapons through successive rescues, advance to next checkpoint
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management (jeep health, prisoner capacity, weapon downgrades on loss) versus forward momentum through hostile territory; balancing offensive capability upgrades against defensive survival
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Elite commando unit infiltrating enemy stronghold in tactical jeep; two-player cooperative military rescue operation with increasing firepower
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Refuses traditional lives/continues structure in favor of gradual weapon degradation mechanic—death costs you tactical capability, not game state. Ties narrative progression (rescuing POWs) directly to mechanical progression (weapon upgrades). Co-op is asymmetric in arcade (one drives, one shoots with rotary joystick) but becomes symmetric in NES port, fundamentally changing tactical options

difficulty.lives_system: Jeep replacement system: player receives additional jeeps at score thresholds; jeep destruction downgrades missile launcher by one level (or fully reverts to grenade launcher in NES version), forcing tactical retreat into weaker loadouts
difficulty.continues_system: Continue feature present; arcade version exploits two-player join-in mechanic to circumvent game-over screen
difficulty.checkpointing: Arcade: one continuous level with no checkpoints. NES: divided into six named checkpoints (Alpha, Baker, Charlie, Delta, Tango, Zulu), each with distinct boss encounter and guaranteed progression point
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High: weapon upgrade path is player-directed through POW extraction strategy. Players choose aggression (risk jeep for upgrades) versus caution (minimize losses). Grenade launcher upgrades to missile launcher then shrapnel variants (2-way, 4-way spread), allowing scaling of offensive capability

progression.upgrade_system: Grenade/missile launcher tier system: base grenade launcher → missile launcher → missile with 2-way shrapnel spread → missile with 4-way shrapnel spread (max three upgrades in NES, four in arcade). Upgrades obtained by: (1) liberating prisoners from solitary camps, or (2) extracting eight POWs in succession to helicopter
progression.unlock_mechanism: Boss encounters gate progression between checkpoints. NES adds six checkpoint bosses not in arcade (missile-spitting statues, battleship, flamethrower tank). Arcade version: single continuous progression with no formal stage gates
replayability_hooks: Score attack: extraction efficiency (successive prisoner batches grant point multipliers)
Weapon optimization: routing decisions affect upgrade availability
Co-op coordination: asymmetric roles in arcade version create different strategic demands than NES symmetric version
Difficulty emergent from progressive weapon downgrades on repeated jeep losses

world_building_techniques: Named four-person squad with character portraits (Colonel Decker, Lieutenant Bob, Sergeant Quint, Corporal Grey) establishes team identity
Mission briefing: rescue POWs from enemy stronghold, destroy ultimate weapon at headquarters
Environmental progression through themed zones (jungle, ruins, lake, hill, mountain, base) suggests advancing into hostile territory
Visual storytelling: prisoners waiting to be picked up after jeep wreckage, helicopter extraction mechanic reinforces rescue narrative
Map screen (NES) shows cumulative progress toward enemy headquarters, reinforcing forward momentum narrative

boss_design.philosophy: Checkpoint finalizers that test current weapon tier and player positioning. NES bosses specifically designed for horizontal arena (battleship rotated 90° to exploit screen width). Final boss (flamethrower tank) counters player's upgraded launcher, forcing machine gun strategy
notable_bosses: Battleship (fires from turrets, repositioned in NES to be 90° rotated with six turrets instead of arcade five)
Final boss: giant flamethrower tank (NES version differs from arcade; machine gun most effective)

faction_indicators: Player squad: Colonel Decker, Lieutenant Bob, Sergeant Quint, Corporal Grey (military hierarchy signaled through ranks)
Enemy forces: implied authoritarian regime holding POWs
POW populations: multiple nationalities/identities (generic prisoners, but extracting them defines heroic narrative)
danger_signals: Enemy internment camps throughout territory (visual marker of occupied zones)
Progressive militarization: enemy vehicles, artillery, statues, helicopter opposition
Ultimate weapon at headquarters: escalating threat justifying offensive capability upgrades
environmental_storytelling: Terrain progression (coast→jungle→ruins→lake→hill→mountain→base) suggests territorial depth and enemy fortification layers
Prisoner distribution across camps indicates systematic detention infrastructure
Helicopter extraction points mark secured positions within hostile territory
Color coding in NES regional version: gates red (Japanese) vs. green (US), final base red (Japanese) vs. brown (US)—minor cosmetic storytelling
